--- Page 1 ---
1
Deep Sleep Scheduling for Satellite IoT via
Simulation-Based Optimization
Wanja de Sombre, Monika Tomov´a, Marek Galinski, Anja Klein, Andrea Ortiz
Abstract—The Satellite Internet of Things (S-IoT) enables
global connectivity for remote sensing devices that must operate
energy-efficiently over long time spans. We consider an S-IoT
system consisting of a sender-receiver pair connected by a
data channel and a feedback channel and capture its dynamics
using a Markov Decision Process (MDP). To extend battery life,
the sender has to decide on deep-sleep durations. Deep-sleep
scheduling is the primary lever to reduce energy consumption,
since sleeping devices consume only a fraction of their idle power.
By choosing its deep-sleep duration online, the sender has to
find a trade-off between energy consumption and data quality
degradation at the receiver, captured by a weighted sum of costs.
We quantify data quality degradation via the recently introduced
Goal-Oriented Tensor (GoT) metric, which can take both age and
content of delivered data into account. We assume a Markovian
observed process and Markov channels with time-varying delay
and erasure rates. The challenge is that content awareness of the
GoT metric makes periodic transmissions inherently inefficient.
Additionally, optimal sleep durations depends on the (unknown)
future states of the observed process and the channels, both of
which must be inferred online. We propose a novel algorithm
using probabilistic simulation-based optimization (PSBO). With
PSBO, the sensor forecasts future states based on estimated tran-
sition probabilities, and uses these forecasts to select the optimal
deep-sleep duration. Extensive simulations and experiments with
S-IoT hardware demonstrate the superior performance of PSBO
under diverse conditions.
Index Terms—Age of Incorrect Information, Satellite Internet
of Things, Status Update Systems, Goal Oriented Tensor, Deep
Sleep Scheduling
I. INTRODUCTION
T
HE Satellite Internet of Things (S-IoT) has emerged as a
solution for enabling the connectivity of sensors even in
remote and infrastructure-sparse regions, where conventional
Internet of Things (IoT) solutions are unavailable. Its potential
extends to a wide range of applications, such as agriculture,
environmental monitoring, and the support of smart grids [14],
Wanja
de
Sombre
(w.sombre@nt.tu-darmstadt.de)
and
Anja
Klein
(a.klein@nt.tu-darmstadt.de)
are
with
the
Communications
Engineer-
ing Lab, Technische Universit¨at Darmstadt, Germany. Monika Tomov´a
(m.tomova@protonmail.com) and Marek Galinski (marek.galinski@stuba.sk)
are affiliated with the Automotive Innovation Lab, Slovak University of
Technology in Bratislava, Slovakia. Andrea Ortiz (andrea.ortiz@tuwien.ac.at)
is with the Institute of Telecommunications, Vienna University of Technology,
Austria.
This work was funded by the BMFTR project Open6GHub+ un-
der grant 16KIS2407, by DAAD with funds from the German Fed-
eral Ministry of Research, Technology and Space BMFTR under grant
57817830,
and
by
the
LOEWE
Center
emergenCITY
under
grant
LOEWE/1/12/519/03/05.001(0016)/72. The work of Andrea Ortiz was
funded by the Vienna Science and Technology Fund WWTF Grant ID
10.47379/VRG23002.
[16]–[18]. These domains often span sparsely populated areas
with intermittent or nonexistent terrestrial coverage, making S-
IoT the only practical means of maintaining continuous, wide-
area sensor connectivity.
Sensors deployed in such remote locations are typically
battery-powered, and it is desirable for them to operate au-
tonomously for as long as possible. This creates a fundamental
tradeoff between their primary function, namely delivering
high quality data, and the goal of conserving energy.
A central strategy for energy-efficient IoT devices is the
optimization of deep-sleep cycles (cf. [2]). In deep-sleep mode,
sensors use only a fraction of the energy they consume when
active or idling. Therefore, optimizing deep-sleep durations is
the primary lever for reducing energy consumption.
At the same time, the device aims to minimize data quality
degradation at the receiver. With data quality, we mean the
application specific usefulness of the data available at the
receiver. Historically, data quality degradation has often been
quantified using the Age of Information (AoI), which was in-
troduced in [19] and has since received significant academic at-
tention (cf. [20]). The AoI only captures data freshness. Hence,
when the AoI is applied to an application whose measured
values remain constant for extended periods and change only
sporadically in abrupt jumps, the same data may be transmitted
repeatedly, incurring avoidable energy consumption. This is
avoided by using the Age of Incorrect Information (AoII)
[21] as a metric for data quality degradation, which measures
the time since the receiver had correct information about the
observed process for the last time. More recently, the Goal-
oriented Tensor (GoT) metric proposed by Li et al. in [22]
offers a further generalization, which we use in this work to
measure data quality degradation. The GoT metric is a function
of the value of one basic data quality degradation metric like
either the AoI or the AoII, the current state of the observed
process and the information at the receiver. Each combination
of these three input values is assigned an application-specific
cost. For example, in a safety-critical system, a critical state
at the observed process that remains unknown to the receiver
for an extended period incurs a substantially higher cost than
a less severe inconsistency or the same inconsistency over a
shorter duration. Together, these costs form the goal-oriented
tensor. In contrast to the AoI, the AoII and the GoT metric
take the content of transmitted data into account. Therefore,
we call the latter metrics content-aware.
In our scenario, the IoT device has to find a tradeoff between
energy consumption and data quality degradation. Energy
consumption and data quality degradation are both associated
arXiv:2602.23788v1  [cs.NI]  27 Feb 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
Reference
Age-Based Metric
Deep-Sleep
Scheduling
Content-Aware
Metric
Minimization of
Energy Consumption
Satellite IoT
Years
[1]–[3]
AoI
✓
✓
2022 - 2024
[4]
AoI
✓
✓
✓
2025
[5]–[9]
AoII
✓
2020 - 2025
[10]–[12]
AoII
✓
✓
2022 - 2023
[13]
AoII
✓
✓
2024
[14]
AoII
✓
✓
✓
2024
[15]
AoII
✓
✓
✓
2024
This work
GoT metric
✓
✓
✓
✓
2026
TABLE I: Summary of related work.
with a cost. The data quality degradation cost is measured via
the GoT metric and the energy consumption cost is measured
in joules. We then minimize a weighted sum of both costs.
When optimizing the described tradeoff between energy
consumption and data quality degradation in S-IoT, solutions
have to take the device’s uncertainty about its environment into
account. The exact behavior of the observed process as well
as channel characteristics are a priori unknown at the device.
Here, channel characteristics differ from those in terrestrial
IoT. The end-to-end S-IoT path spans multiple time-varying
links (sensor to satellite terminal, satellite terminal to satellite,
inter-satellite, satellite to ground station, and ground stations
to server). Additionally, Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite
constellations are highly dynamic and continually change link
geometry and visibility. Therefore, path loss, delay, and outage
probability can fluctuate strongly over time [16], [23], [24].
We consider a battery-powered IoT device that observes
a process such as electric current, temperature, acceleration,
humidity or light intensity, and transmits measurements to a
remote receiver via a satellite link. Time is modeled in discrete
steps, during which the device can either be awake or in deep-
sleep mode. When awake, it senses the current environment
and transmits the obtained measurements. New measurements
are only transmitted if the transmission improves the data
quality at the receiver. We model the diverse time-varying
channels as one joint Markov channel. With this model, we
assume that the channels are packet erasure channels, where
erasure rate and delay change according to a Markov process.
To reduce energy consumption, the device may enter a deep-
sleep phase for multiple time steps, during which it is entirely
inactive and cannot respond to acknowledgments or changes
in the environment. The central decision of the IoT device is
whether to enter deep sleep and, if so, for how long. When
the expected changes in the measured values are moderate,
longer deep-sleep durations are possible. In contrast, high
anticipated fluctuations in measurements require shorter deep-
sleep intervals.
We propose the PSBO approach, a Probabilistic Simulation-
based Optimization (cf. [25]): For each deep-sleep duration,
in ascending order, the probability of every possible time-
averaged cost is estimated by using a simulation of the
system during deep-sleep. The device then uses these estimates
to calculate expected time-averaged costs for the deep-sleep
durations and to optimize the deep-sleep duration by choosing
the one with the lowest expected time-averaged cost. To carry
out these simulations, the device is dependent on knowledge
about the environment, e.g. about the observed process and the
channels. It obtains this knowledge by observing the observed
process and the channels over time and thereby gradually
improves the simulations of different deep-sleep durations.
Our approach exploits two problem-specific properties of
deep-sleep duration selection. First, instead of a sample-based
approach with many simulations, we use a distribution-based
simulation. Rather than generating many samples and aver-
aging their outcomes, we propagate belief distributions over
the costs and compute expected time-averaged costs directly
from these distributions. This enables a substantially more
efficient evaluation of candidate deep-sleep durations. Second,
we exploit the fact that the distribution-based simulation for
a deep-sleep duration of n + 1 steps can build directly on the
distribution-based simulation for a deep-sleep duration of n
steps. Consequently, evaluating a sequence of candidate deep-
sleep durations does not require restarting the simulation from
scratch for each duration, but can be carried out via a single
progressively extended simulation.
The contributions of this paper are as follows:
• We formulate the problem of energy-efficient deep-sleep
scheduling for a sender-receiver pair in S-IoT as a
Markov Decision Process (MDP). In this MDP, actions
correspond to deciding whether the device enters deep-
sleep mode and, if so, for how long. Whenever the S-IoT
device is awake, it has to determine its next deep-sleep
duration to minimize a weighted sum of costs derived
from the GoT metric and energy costs.
• In the formulated MDP, we model the device’s current
belief, which serves as the basis for the device’s decision
about its next deep-sleep duration. This belief consists of
all the information, which the device learned previously
about the channel conditions and the observed process.
• We propose a novel PSBO algorithm for optimizing these
decisions on deep-sleep durations. PSBO uses learned
knowledge about the observed process and the channels


--- Page 3 ---
3
to carry out simulations for different deep-sleep durations.
Based on the expected time-averaged cost values obtained
through these simulations, PSBO decides on the next
deep-sleep duration.
• Through extensive numerical simulations, we analyze
PSBO’s behavior under varying conditions and show
its superior performance compared to baselines, such as
threshold-based solutions and Q-learning.
• We validate the feasibility and effectiveness of our ap-
proach through a prototype implementation on real S-IoT
hardware, demonstrating its practical applicability.
The paper is organized as follows: Section II reviews related
work. Section III introduces the system model, and Section IV
formulates the optimization problem. Our approach, PSBO, is
described in Section V. In Section VII we present numerical
and experimental results, respectively, with implementation
details in Section VI. Section VIII concludes the paper.
II. RELATED WORK
This section reviews related work on deep-sleep scheduling
for minimizing energy consumption and for content-aware,
age-based metrics in the S-IoT. We categorize and summarize
existing works and conclude this section by describing how
our approach differs from the state of the art.
Table I compares the studies discussed in this section across
the above-mentioned criteria. Broadly, the literature falls into
two groups. First, studies that optimize deep-sleep scheduling
but do not use a content-aware age-based metric [1]–[4]. Sec-
ond, studies that employ the AoII, a content-aware age-based
metric, but do not address deep-sleep scheduling [5]–[14]. The
authors of [15] offer first insights into the combination of both
lines of research.
We begin with the first group. The optimization of deep-
sleep intervals has primarily been studied in the context of
AoI [1]–[4], where only the authors of [4] explicitly consider
the S-IoT and its distinctive channel conditions in their model.
Specifically, in [1], deep-sleep schedules in the Industrial
Internet of Things (IIoT) are optimized using Proximal Policy
Optimization. The authors of [2] use an MDP to derive an
optimal cyclic deep-sleep schedule, offering a closed-form so-
lution for the AoI-energy tradeoff. In [3], deep-sleep intervals
are scheduled for unreliable channels using MDP-based meth-
ods, providing a two-threshold optimal policy with a closed-
form expression for the AoI-energy tradeoff. The authors of
[4] schedule transmissions in the S-IoT to minimize the AoI
over a finite time horizon. While the above-cited papers make
essential contributions to optimizing deep-sleep schedules for
age-based metrics, these approaches focus exclusively on
minimizing information age and do not consider a content-
aware metric, which can lead to unnecessary transmissions.
In recent years, a new line of research has emerged around
the AoII (also referred to as Age of Synchronisation (AoS)),
introduced in [21]. Unlike AoI, the AoII accounts for the
age of information and also for the similarity between the
sender’s and receiver’s information. The AoII has been studied
in various scenarios. In [5], we minimize the AoII in energy-
harvesting IoT systems by learning transmission schedules
through a Finite Difference Policy Gradient (FDPG) method.
Similarly, in [6] and [7], the authors model unreliable channels
or channels with random delay and develop threshold-based
scheduling strategies using constrained or standard Markov
decision processes. These works prove the optimality of
simple policies under certain system conditions. In [8], the
authors minimize the AoII for a binary Markov source, using
dynamic programming to derive optimal sampling policies.
The authors of [10], [11] investigate the trade-off between
energy consumption and AoII. We analyze the same trade-
off in our previous work [12]. The authors of [13] and [14]
consider transmission scheduling to optimize the AoII in the
context of the S-IoT. Beyond the transmitter side, recent works
also explore optimization at the receiver or network level.
All the authors of these works provide important insights
into minimizing the AoII, but do not optimize deep-sleep
intervals. Only in [15], deep-sleep intervals are optimized to
minimize both AoII and energy consumption using threshold-
based methods. However, the authors assume a fixed cycle
length, which is suboptimal. Furthermore, the work focuses on
minimizing energy consumption subject to an AoII constraint,
rather than considering a more flexible weighted sum of data
quality degradation and energy consumption.
The concept of the AoII was further generalized in [22],
where Li et al. introduced the GoT metric. This metric allows
to use application-specific weights to scale costs of data quality
degradation depending on a basic data quality degradation
metric like either the AoI or the AoII, the current state of
the observed process and the information at the receiver. By
using the GoT metric for deep-sleep scheduling, we extend and
generalize the discussed line of research on age-based metrics
in the IoT.
To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to char-
acterize and find a tradeoff between data quality degradation,
quantified by the GoT metric, and energy consumption via
deep-sleep scheduling in the S-IoT. It is also the first to apply
probabilistic simulation-based optimization in this context by
exploiting the problem-specific structure of the deep-sleep
scheduling task to enable an efficient implementation.
III. SYSTEM MODEL
Fig. 1 illustrates the main components of the considered sys-
tem. It consists of a battery-powered IoT device that transmits
environmental measurements via a multi-hop connection to a
receiving server (called receiver). The link between the IoT
device and the receiver comprises a wireless connection to a
satellite terminal, the wireless link from the satellite terminal
to a satellite of the satellite constellation, inter-satellite-links
within the constellation, the connection from a satellite to a
ground station, and the connection between ground station and
receiver.
We consider an infinite time horizon, divided into discrete
time steps t = 0, 1, . . . , each of the same duration T. As
indicated in Fig. 2, the IoT device is in one of two possible
modes, either awake or in deep-sleep. If it is awake, it can use
its sensors, power its antenna, and decide to switch to deep-
sleep mode. If the device decides to sleep after time step t,


--- Page 4 ---
4
it specifies a number N sleep
t
of deep-sleep time steps and sets
a timer. Then, after N sleep
t
time steps, the device wakes up
automatically.
In a single time step in which the device is in awake mode,
it progresses through the following phases in a fixed order
as indicated in Fig. 2. All phases are assumed to have fixed
duration, except for the Listening-Phase whose length depends
on when feedback is received:
• Wake-up Phase: The IoT device powers up its core
components and prepares for operation.
• Sensing-Phase: The device uses its sensor to sample the
observed process and stores the new measurement in its
buffer.
• Transmission-Phase: The device uses its antenna to trans-
mit the new measurement.
• Listening-Phase: The device waits for feedback from the
receiver. In case no ACK is received in this phase, the
device assumes the transmission failed.
• Idle-Phase: After feedback is received, the device turns
off its antenna and waits until the beginning of the next
time step. If the device has decided to sleep in the next
time step, this phase is skipped and the device transitions
directly to deep-sleep mode. This transition is assumed
to be practically instantaneous, so no separate fall-asleep
phase is modeled.
In deep-sleep mode, there are no distinct phases.
The IoT device observes an environmental process such as
electric current, temperature, acceleration, humidity or light
intensity. This process is shown on the left in Fig. 1. The
dynamics of this process are captured by a Markov Chain X,
which is assumed to be stationary. The process state in time
step t is denoted by Xt. We represent the observed process by
a discrete state space. Therefore, physical values are quantized
into one of the finitely many states in X, and Xt denotes the
resulting quantized process state. The evolution of Xt follows
the transition probabilities given by the matrix P proc, which
is defined as:
P proc(X, X′) := P(Xt+1 = X′ | Xt = X).
(1)
To measure the observed process, the IoT device uses its
sensors and stores the latest measurement in its buffer. The
measurement in the device’s buffer in time step t are denoted
by XTx
t
.
After sensing, the IoT device transmits the new measure-
ment over a multi-hop connection, outlined as solid arrows
in Fig. 1, to the receiver. We call this connection the data
channel. In case the age-based metric F used as input for
the GoT metric is the AoI, we assume that the IoT device
transmits every new measurement. In case F is the AoII, the
device transmits a new measurement only if it differs from
the information currently available at the receiver. As shown
in Fig. 1, the transmission starts with the device forwarding
its buffered measurement to a satellite terminal. From this
satellite terminal, the measurement is sent to a satellite of
a LEO satellite constellation using Ka-Band frequencies. The
measurement is then directly transmitted to a terrestrial ground
station over Ku-Band or indirectly via optical inter-satellite
links through another satellite. Finally, the ground station
delivers the measurement via a fiber-optic connection to the
receiving server. The most recently received measurement at
the receiver in time step t is denoted by XRx
t
.
In case the data reaches the receiver successfully, an ac-
knowledgment signal is transmitted via the feedback channel
to the IoT device. In Fig. 1, the feedback channel is outlined
as dotted arrows. The acknowledgment is routed back to the
IoT device by retracing the same transmission steps as the
measurement data, but in reverse order. Note that while the
transmission steps are mirrored, the physical route may differ.
We model the data channel from the IoT device to the
receiver as a Markov channel (cf. [26]). A Markov channel
models the channel as a packet erasure channel with a packet
erasure rate and a delay changing according to a Markov
chain. Every channel state Cdata ∈Cdata is a tuple of two
values Cdata = (Cdelay
data , Cerasure
data
), where the first value,
Cdelay
data , models the channel’s delay in seconds. The second
value, Cerasure
data
, models the channel’s erasure rate. Whenever
the IoT device attempts to transmit a measurement, the data
reaches with a probability of 1 −Cerasure
data
, and is erased with
a probability of Cerasure
data
.
We model the state of the feedback channel separately from
that of the data channel as Cfeedback = (Cdelay
feedback, Cerasure
feedback).
We model their dynamics using the Markov chain C with
states C = (Cdata, Cfeedback). The transition probabilities for
this Markov chain are given by the matrix P channel defined
by:
P channel(C, C′) := P(Ct+1 = C′|Ct = C).
(2)
Note that although the states of the two channels can correlate,
they are not the same.
The device operates on battery power, and each action
draws electrical power at a different level. Waking up from
deep-sleep has a time duration T w (in seconds) and draws
power P w. Sensing has a time duration T s (in seconds) and
draws power P s. Transmitting and receiving feedback requires
activating the antenna and draws power P a. Deep-sleep mode
incurs a minimal baseline power draw P d, and idling incurs
a power draw P i.
The device aims to balance its energy consumption and
the data quality degradation at the receiver. Here, the latter is
measured using the GoT metric [22]. The GoT metric builds
on classical age-based metrics like either the AoI or the AoII.
We first define these age-based metrics before providing a
definition for the GoT metric.
With AoI0 = 0, the AoI at time step t is recursively defined
as
AoIt+1 =
(
0,
if successfully transmitted,
AoIt + 1,
otherwise.
(3)
The AoII combines both freshness and correctness, and it
is defined recursively as
AoIIt+1 =
(
AoIIt + 1,
if Xt ̸= XRx
t
,
0,
otherwise,
(4)
with AoII0 = 0. Assuming that after M time steps, data is
outdated in the sense that the data quality will not further


--- Page 5 ---
5
Satellite 
Link
Receiver
Observed 
Process
Measurement 
Value
Data Channel
Feedback Channel
IoT 
Device
Battery
Satellite 
Terminal
LEO Satellite 
Constellation
Ground 
Station
Fig. 1: System Model
decrease, we use a cap M ∈N for age-based metrics, meaning
that instead of increasing indefinitely, these metrics grow until
they reach the value M and stay constant at M afterwards.
The GoT metric is defined as a mapping
GoT : X × X × {0, . . . , M} →R,
(5)
where X denotes the space of possible process states, and
M is the maximum value of the considered age-based metric
F. Notably, widely-used metrics such as Mean Squared Error
(MSE), AoI, and AoII can be represented as special cases of
the GoT.
To illustrate the advantages of the GoT, we provide a
detailed example:
Example 1. In safety-critical systems, certain mismatches
are more dangerous than others. Consider the case where
Xt ∈{Safe, Warning, Emergency}, and the receiver
state XRx
t
∈{Safe, Warning, Emergency}. Then we can
define the function GoT as follows:
GoT(Xt, XRx
t
, AoIt) =α · 1{Xt=Emergency∧XRx
t
=Safe}
+ βAoIt,
where α ≫β assigns a high penalty if the IoT device
detects an emergency, but the receiver is unaware, which is
particularly hazardous.
IV. PROBLEM FORMULATION
The goal of the IoT device is to find a tradeoff between
its energy consumption and data quality degradation at the
receiver. To this end, the device decides whether to enter deep-
sleep mode and, if so, for how long. This decision-making
process is based on the available information that the device
continuously collects and utilizes. We formulate this decision-
making problem as an MDP M = (S, A, c, P) consisting of a
set S of states, a set A of actions, a cost function c : S ×A →
R, and transition probabilities P : S × A × S →[0, 1]. In this
section, we define the components of this MDP and derive the
corresponding optimization problem.
To define the states of M, we first define the notion of a
belief at the IoT device. In time step t, the belief Bt ∈B is
a tuple consisting of
ad
Phases
Awake
Awake
Awake
Awake
Awake
Time Step 
𝑡
Time Step 
𝑡−1
Time Step 
𝑡+ 1
Wake-up
Sensing
Transmission
Listening
𝑇s
𝑇w
𝑇a
𝑇
Time Steps
𝑡= 0
𝑇
Deep-Sleep
Deep-Sleep
Deep-Sleep
Deep-Sleep
Deep-Sleep
𝑇0sleep
𝑇5sleep
𝑇15sleep
𝑇21sleep
𝑇28sleep
Modes
Idle / Deep-Sleep
Fig. 2: Overview of the device’s operation schedule. Top: Al-
ternation between deep-sleep and awake mode across multiple
time steps. Bottom: Detailed view of a single time step.
• the state XTx
t
∈X in the device’s buffer, representing
the most recently sensed measurement from the observed
process,
• estimated transition probabilities P proc,est
t
: X × X →
[0, 1] of the observed process,
• the current belief distribution DX
t
: X →[0, 1] over the
states of the observed process,
• the belief distribution DXRx
t
: X →[0, 1] over the states
of the observed process most recently received at the
receiver,
• the belief distribution of the age-based metric used as
input for the GoT, which is either
– the belief distribution DAoIRx
t
: (0, ..., M) →[0, 1]
over the AoI at the receiver, or
– the tensor T AoII
t
: X × X × (0, ..., M) →[0, 1],
containing the device’s knowledge about the current
AoII depending on X and XRx,
• the sleep duration N sleep
t
∈N0 indicating the number
of time steps the device will stay in deep-sleep before
waking up, and
• the last time step tawake
t
during which the device was
awake.
Using this definition of a belief, we define a state S =
(C, X, XTx, XRx, AoITx, AoIRx, AoII, B) ∈S. It consists
of:
• a channel state C ∈C,
• a state X ∈X of the observed process,
• a state XRx ∈X most recently received at the receiver,
• an age of information AoIRx ∈(0, ..., M) at the receiver,


--- Page 6 ---
6
• an age of incorrect information AoII ∈(0, ..., M), and
• a belief B ∈B at the IoT device as described above.
The state of the MDP at time step t, denoted by St, consists
of the same components, each indexed by t.
The set A = {0, 1, . . . , amax} of actions consists of all
possible deep-sleep intervals (at = 0 denotes staying awake).
at specifies the duration of the deep-sleep after time step t.
To determine action at ∈A in time step t, the device can
only access the information in Bt. A strategy π is called a
belief-based strategy, if it is a mapping π : B →A.
The transition probabilities P of M are given by the
dynamics described in Section III and can be summarized as
follows: The evolution of Xt and Ct, given by (1) and (2), is
assumed to be stochastically independent. XRx is updated in
case of a successful transmission as
XRx
t+1 =
(
XTx
t+1
if successfully transmitted,
XRx
t
otherwise.
(6)
Whether a transmission attempt is successful depends on the
current data channel quality Cerasure
data,t
as described in Section
III. AoIRx
t
and AoIIt are updated according to (3) and (4).
The transition from Bt to Bt+1 is outlined in Alg. 1, which
is described in detail in Sec. V.
Energy costs are defined as:
ce(St) = T w
t ·P w +T s
t ·P s +T a
t ·P a +T i
t ·P i +T d
t ·P d, (7)
where T w
t
is the time spent waking up in time step t. If the
device was already awake in the previous time step t −1,
then T w
t
= 0. T s
t , T a
t , T i
t and T d
t
represent the time the
device spends using sensors, powering the antenna, idling and
remaining in deep-sleep, respectively. The sum of all durations
per time step is constant: T w
t + T s
t + T a
t + T d
t = T.
Without new transmissions, the data quality at the receiver
degrades. The cost incurred by this degradation is given by:
cqual(St) = cqual((X, XTx, XRx, AoITx, AoIRx, AoIIRx))
= GoT(X, XRx, F),
(8)
where F is either AoIRx or AoIIRx.
Together, we define the cost function c : S →R of M as
a weighted sum of energy consumption cost and data quality
degradation cost:
c(St) = we · ce(St) + wqual · cqual(St).
(9)
Here, we, wqual ∈R are weights that define the relative
importance of energy consumption and data quality in the
device’s optimization objective.
Using the defined terms, the average cost of a strategy π
can be expressed as
cπ
avg =
lim
tfinal→∞
"
1
tfinal
tfinal−1
X
t=0
c(Sπ
t )
#
,
(10)
where the states Sπ
t are obtained using M and π. Accordingly,
we define
cπ
qual =
lim
tfinal→∞
"
1
tfinal
tfinal−1
X
t=0
cqual(Sπ
t )
#
(11)
Alg. 1: BeliefUpdate
Alg. 2: TensorUpdate
update belief
Alg. 4: PSBO
Alg. 3: PredictCost
simulate step t + n
loop over  
convexity check
return sleep duration 
Fig. 3: Overview of the interaction between Alg. 1 to 4.
and
cπ
e =
lim
tfinal→∞
"
1
tfinal
tfinal−1
X
t=0
ce(Sπ
t )
#
.
(12)
With cπ
avg, the optimization problem can be written as:
min
π∈Π E[cπ
avg].
(13)
Consequently, we refer to the optimized quantity as expected
time-averaged cost.
V. PROBABILISTIC SIMULATION-BASED OPTIMIZATION
In this section, we introduce PSBO, our approach for
finding the optimal tradeoff between data quality degradation
at the receiver and the energy consumption of the IoT device.
To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to apply this
type of algorithm based on probabilistic simulations to treat
the tradeoff of energy consumption and age-based metrics for
data quality degradation. For an overview over our solution
approach, we provide a flowchart including the involved algo-
rithms in Fig. 3. Following the procedure over the course of a
single time step t, we describe these algorithms in Sec. V-A
to Sec. V-C. In Sec. V-A, we describe Alg. 1, which performs
the belief update process at the IoT device. For this update, the
device uses Alg. 2, which is the sub-procedure to update the
device’s belief distribution of the AoII. The resulting updated
belief is then passed to Alg. 4, which simulates the system
for deep-sleep durations of increasing length n and uses Alg.
3 in every iteration to estimate the expected time-averaged
cost. Alg. 4 executes a convexity check after every iteration
and returns the optimal deep-sleep duration n as soon as
the increase in expected time-averaged cost from data quality
degradation exceeds the decrease in expected time-averaged
energy cost. We describe PSBO in Sec. V-C after explaining
Alg. 3 in Sec. V-B.
A. Belief Update
Algorithm 1 describes the procedure for updating the IoT
device’s belief from Bt−1 to Bt at time step t. Here, we update
every component of the belief as listed in Section III. Lines
1–3 check whether the device was in deep-sleep mode during
time step t. If it was, the buffered measurement XTx
t
remains
unchanged compared to XTx
t−1, and the device updates its belief


--- Page 7 ---
7
Algorithm 1: BeliefUpdate
Input
: Belief Bt−1
Output
: Updated belief Bt
1 if tawake
t−1
+ Nsleep
t−1
> t then
2
XTx
t
←XTx
t−1
3
DX
t
←DX
t−1 · P proc,est
t−1
4 else
5
XTx
t
←Xt
6
P proc,est
t
←t · P proc,est
t−1
7
if XTx
t−1 = Xt then
8
P proc,est
t
(XTx
t−1, XTx
t−1) ←
P proc,est
t−1
(XTx
t−1, XTx
t−1) + Nsleep
t−1
+ 1
9
else
10
P proc,est
t
(XTx
t−1, XTx
t−1) ←
P proc,est
t−1
(XTx
t−1, XTx
t−1) + ⌊Nsleep
t−1 /2⌋
11
P proc,est
t
(XTx
t−1, XTx
t
) ←P proc,est
t−1
(XTx
t−1, XTx
t
) + 1
12
P proc,est
t
(XTx
t
, XTx
t
) ←
P proc,est
t−1
(XTx
t
, XTx
t
) + Nsleep
t−1
−⌊Nsleep
t−1 /2⌋
13
Normalize P proc,est
t
row-wise
14
DX
t
←(0, . . . , 0)
15
DX
t (XTx
t
) ←1
16
DXRx
t
←(0, . . . , 0)
17
DXRx
t
(XRx
t
) ←1
18
tawake
t−1
←t
19 if F = AoI then
20
DAoIRx
t
←(0, . . . , 0)
21
DAoIRx
t
(AoIRx
t
) ←1
22 else if F = AoII then
23
T AoII
t
←TensorUpdate(Bt−1, DX
t )
24 return Bt
distribution DX
t about the environmental process by multiply-
ing with the estimated transition probabilities P proc,est
t−1
. This
way, we simulate one step of the observed process using the
knowledge gathered over time in the form of the estimated
transition probabilities.
Lines 4–17 correspond to the case when the device is
awake and performs sensing: the new measurement is stored
in the buffer (line 5), and the estimated transition probabilities
P proc,est
t−1
are updated based on the observed state transitions
during deep-sleep mode (lines 6–13). This update assumes that
at most one state transition occurred during the sleep period.
This assumption does not necessarily hold true. However, for
fast changing processes, our solution tends to choose shorter
deep-sleep intervals, which reduced potential errors. Alterna-
tively, it is possible to initially spend energy to stay awake for
a certain period of time to estimate the transition probabilities
of the observed process without using the additional above-
mentioned assumption. In lines 14-17, the belief distributions
DX
t
and DXRx
t
are reset, by setting the probability to the
current states to 1. Line 18 marks the current time as the last
awake time step. If the age-based metric F used as the input
for the GoT is the AoI, we update the AoI distribution DAoIRx
t−1
according to Eq. 3 (line 20 and 21). Both in line 17 and 21,
the device only assumes that the transmission was successful
in case it received an ACK over the feedback channel.
If F is the AoII, line 23 updates the tensor T AoII
t−1 , which
captures the distributions over the AoII. This tensor update
Algorithm 2: TensorUpdate
Input
: Belief Bt−1, Updated Distribution DX
t
Output
: Updated Tensor Tt
1 T AoII
t
←0
2 foreach X ∈X do
3
if DX
t−1(X) > 0 then
4
foreach X′ ∈X do
5
R ←(0, . . . , 0)
6
for k = 0 to M −1 do
7
R(k + 1) ←T AoII
t−1 (X, X′, k)
8
R(M) ←R(M) + T AoII
t−1 (X, X′, M)
9
foreach X′′ ∈X do
10
if DX
t (X′′) > 0 then
11
if X′′ ̸= X′ then
12
if DX
t (X′′) = 1 then
13
for ∆= 0 to M do
14
T AoII
t
(X′′, X′, ∆) +=
DX
t−1(X) · R(∆)
15
else
16
for ∆= 0 to M do
17
T AoII
t
(X′′, X′, ∆) +=
DX
t−1(X)
DX
t (X′′) ·
P proc,est
t−1
(X, X′′) · R(∆)
18
else
19
T AoII
t
(X′′, X′, 0) ←1
20 return T AoII
t
is performed using Alg. 2. The updated belief Bt is returned
in line 24. To update its belief, the IoT device performs this
update N sleep
t−1
times after waking up.
Next, we explain Alg. 2, which is used in line 20 of Alg.
1 and updates the device’s belief distribution T AoII
t
for the
AoII. To update every entry of the tensor T AoII
t
, we must
consider every possible transition in the process state. We
iterate over all possible previous process states X, all possible
states X′ at the receiver and all possible current states X′′.
Lastly, we have to iterate over all possible values ∆of the
AoII. In the following, we explain this update-procedure line
by line. For each possible process state X, Algorithm 2 first
checks whether the probability to be in X is estimated to be
greater than 0 (lines 2-3). If it is, T AoII is updated for every
state X′ at the receiver (line 4). As the AoII increases by 1
whenever the information at the receiver is wrong, in lines
5-8, all distributions are shifted to the right and stored in a
temporary variable R. In lines 9-19, the AoII distribution for
every process state X′′ and state X′ at the receiver is updated,
whenever the estimated probability for the observed process
to be in state X′′ is positive. If X′′ = X′, the information
at the receiver is correct, the AoII is 0 and the respective
AoII distribution is set accordingly (line 18-19). If X′′ ̸= X′,
T AoII
t
is increased by the probability of the observed process
to evolve from X in time step t to X′′ in time step t weighted
by R(∆) for every AoII ∆(lines 12-17). Finally, the algorithm
returns the updated tensor T AoII
t
in line 20.


--- Page 8 ---
8
Algorithm 3: PredictCost
Input
: Belief Bt, Goal-oriented Tensor GoT
Output
: Predicted Cost
1 if F = AoI then
2
cost ←
X
X∈X
X′∈X
∆=0,...,M
DX
t (X)DXRx
t
(X′)DAoIRx
t
(∆)GoT(X, X′, ∆)
3 else if F = AoII then
4
cost ←
X
X∈X
X′∈X
∆=0,...,M
DX
t (X)DXRx
t
(X′)(T AoII
t
· GoT)(X, X′, ∆)
5 return cost
B. Cost Prediction
To predict the expected cost in a specific time step t, we
use Algorithm 3, which determines the expected cost in time
step t based on the GoT and Bt. If the age-based metric F
used as input for the GoT metric is the AoI, the expected
cost is calculated as specified in line 2. If F is the AoII, the
expected cost is obtained as specified in line 4. In both cases,
every possible combination of states X of the process, X′ at
the receiver, and ages ∆is considered by summing over the
belief distribution weighted by the goal-oriented tensor.
C. PSBO
Algorithm 4 determines the optimal deep-sleep duration
through a simulation-based optimization of the expected time-
averaged cost1. In ascending order, the algorithm considers
possible deep-sleep durations and estimates the corresponding
expected time-averaged cost based on the device’s current
belief. These estimates are high for short deep-sleep durations,
because of high energy cost. As the deep-sleep duration
increases, the total cost initially decreases because the energy
cost drops. Beyond a certain point, however, longer deep-sleep
periods start to degrade the data quality at the receiver. Once
the cost of data quality degradation becomes larger than the
decrease in energy cost, the overall cost begins to increase
again. At this point, the algorithm has found the optimal
duration for the upcoming deep-sleep. In the following, we
explain the steps of this procedure line by line.
As inputs, PSBO needs all parameters concerning en-
ergy consumption and cost, namely we, wqual, T w, P w,
T s, P s,P a,P d,and P i, as well as a maximum deep-sleep
duration, the current belief at the device, and a maximum
number of steps used for transmission attempts after wak-
ing up. Lines 1–5 initialize variables: the optimal deep-
sleep duration best, accumulated data quality degradation cost
acc dat cost, previously computed expected time-averaged
cost prev avg cost, the deep-sleep duration counter N sleep
t
,
and the current belief B. Here, we use a B for the device’s
1A full python-based implementation will be shared after the review process
is completed.
Algorithm 4: PSBO
Input
: Belief Bt,
max sleep (max. sleep duration),
max tx steps (max. no of Tx steps),
we,wqual,T w, P w, T s, P s,P a,P d, P i.
Output
: Optimal sleep duration
1 best ←0
2 acc dat cost ←0
3 prev avg cost ←∞
4 Nsleep
t
←∞
5 B ←Bt
6 while best < max sleep do
7
total time ←best
8
avg cost ←acc dat cost
total time
9
B′ ←B
10
for tx step ∈{1, . . . , max tx steps} do
11
p ←(1 −rt) · rtx step−1
t
12
B′ ←BeliefUpdate(B′)
13
dat cost ←wqual · PredictCost(B′)
14
e cost ←we · (T wP w + T sP s + T a
t+best+tx stepP a +
T iP i + T d
t+best+tx stepP d)
15
avg cost ←total time·avg cost+p·(dat cost+e cost)
total time+p
16
total time ←total time + p
17
end
18
if avg cost > prev avg cost then
19
best ←best −1
20
break
21
prev avg cost ←avg cost
22
best ←best + 1
23
B ←BeliefUpdate(B)
24
acc dat cost ←acc dat cost + PredictCost(B)
25 end
26 Nsleep
t
←best
27 return best
belief during the simulation. Lines 6–25 iterate to find the
optimal deep-sleep interval. The loop is terminated if (a)
this optimal deep-sleep interval is found (line 20) or (b) a
maximal sleep duration max sleep is reached (line 6). In the
latter case, the IoT device sleeps for max sleep steps, i.e.,
N sleep
t
= max sleep. Within each iteration, lines 7-9 are used
to initialize the current total duration total time, the expected
time-averaged cost avg cost, and to copy the current belief
B to B′. Starting from this belief B′, the simulation for the
transmission steps after the deep-sleep interval of duration
best starts in line 10.
We simulate a predefined number of max tx steps trans-
mission time steps. During each transmission step, we first
calculate the probability that the device needs exactly tx step
time steps for the transmission in line 11. At this point,
the algorithm uses rt
= Cdata
erasure,t · Cfeedback
erasure,t, which is
the probability to receive an ACK after a transmission in
time step t. To estimate rt, the IoT device tracks previous
transmission attempts and calculates a mean by dividing
the number of successful transmission attempts by the total
number of transmission attempts. Given rt, the probability
that the transmission succeeds after exactly tx step time steps
is (1 −rt) · rtx step−1
t
. Line 12 updates the belief B′ used
during the simulation of the transmission phase. In line 13,
the cost cqual resulting from data quality degradation in the
current transmission time step are predicted through Algorithm


--- Page 9 ---
9
3. Line 14 computes the energy cost (e cost) of waking up,
sensing, and transmitting, weighted by we according to Eq. 7.
The probability P Tx(B) to transmit given belief B depends
on the metric F. The average required time T a
t during which
the antenna is powered during transmission time step t is
approximated by
T a
t = min(T, rt · Cdelay + (1 −rt) · T a
rt
),
(14)
where Cdelay = Cdata
delay + Cfeedback
delay
is the average delay
between the transmission of a measurement and the reception
of an ACK at the device, which the IoT device estimates
as a mean from round-trip times obtained through feedback
from previous transmission attempts. In line 15, the expected
time-averaged cost avg cost is computed. Line 16 increases
the total time by an additional expected duration p, which
is the number of transmission time steps that are required
on average to successfully transmit. Lines 18–20 implement
a convexity check: once the expected time-averaged cost
begins to increase, the previous deep-sleep duration is optimal,
assuming the entries in the GoT are monotone in the sense that
as long as the device does not transmit after some time step
t0 ∈N
∀t, t′ > t0, X, X′, X′′, X′′′ ∈X :
t < t′ ⇒GoT(X, X′, Ft) ≤GoT(X′′, X′′′, Ft′).
A weaker and still sufficient assumption for optimality is
that the sequence of expected data degradation costs cqual
is convex. If none of these conditions is satisfied, longer
deep-sleep durations with lower expected time-averaged costs
may exist. In this case, our approach conservatively returns
the first local minimum. After finding the minimum, the
algorithm terminates. Otherwise, the loop continues, updating
prev avg cost, best, B, and acc dat cost accordingly (lines
21–24).
Finally, the algorithm sets the optimal sleep duration N sleep
t
in line 26 and returns it in line 27.
VI. HARDWARE IMPLEMENTATION
To validate the applicability of the proposed PSBO ap-
proach under realistic conditions, a status update system is
implemented. The physical setup follows the structure of the
system model from Section III, with one additional compo-
nent, namely, the control station (a Linux-based laptop running
a Python script) which replaces the physical sensing process to
allow reproducible experiments. The control station addition-
ally serves as a logging interface. Apart from this substitution,
the interactions between elements reproduce the logical flow
of the modeled status update system. Apart from the control
station, we use a sensor node transmitting to a Bluetooth Low
Energy (BLE) gateway, which receives measurements from
the sensor node and is connected to a Starlink user terminal.
This user terminal is used to connect the setup via the Starlink
constellation to a remote server (a Linux host running a Python
script). Fig. 4 shows the physical layout of the experimental
setup. The local components were deployed in Bratislava,
Slovakia, while the remote server was hosted in Germany.
Sensor Node
BLE Gateway
Control Station
Fig. 4: Physical layout of the experimental setup consisting of
the control station, sensor node, and BLE gateway.
The hardware implementation of the experimental system
relies on widely used ESP32-based development boards and
standard computing devices. The sensor node is built on the
FIIT ESP32 Kit [27], a custom development board designed at
the Slovak University of Technology in Bratislava (FIIT STU)
for research in IoT applications.
In the theoretical model, the IoT device measures the
environmental process directly. In the experimental setup, the
corresponding measurements were taken offline, are stored on
the control station and transmitted to the sensor node over
a USB serial link. The node treats the received value as its
current observation Xt, stores it as XT x
t
, and uses it for belief
updates and decision-making as described in previous sec-
tions. The measurements correspond to light-intensity values
collected during one hour of car driving around Bratislava.
These values are used to model Markov chains to reproduce
realistic environmental transitions and extended to a 24-hour
time horizon by repeated sampling from the learned process
model. Using externally provided traces makes it possible to
evaluate different agents under identical conditions, since all of
them operate on the same sequence of environmental values.
The IoT device used in the implementation is an ESP32-S3-
based microcontroller, which provides the required interfaces
for serial communication, BLE transmission, and deep-sleep
operation. These capabilities allow the device to implement
the behavior defined in Section III. The communication chain
corresponds to the multi-hop link in Fig. 1, with additional
communication between the control station and the sensor
node. The BLE gateway is implemented on an ESP32-based
development board that provides both BLE and Ethernet
connectivity. BLE enables the short-range connection between
the sensor node and the gateway, while the gateway forwards
packets to the remote server via an Ethernet link routed
through the Starlink network. Acknowledgment messages al-
low the sensor node to update its beliefs about XRx, AoIRx,
AoII and the channel behavior based on observed outcomes.
The firmware running on the microcontrollers is imple-
mented in C/C++ using the PlatformIO development envi-
ronment. It uses the Arduino framework built on top of the


--- Page 10 ---
10
Parameter
Description
Value
Range
N
number of
repetitions
100
-
tfinal
number of
time steps
per repetition
86, 400
-
T
time step duration
1s
-
T Tx
transmission duration
64 ms
-
T w
wake up duration
47 ms
-
T s
sensing duration
52 ms (temperatures)
0 ms (light intensities)
-
Cdelay
data
data channel delay
40 - 220 ms
-
Cdelay
feedback
feedback
channel delay
40 - 220 ms
-
Cerasure
feedback
feedback channel
erasure rate
0.00
-
wqual
data quality weight
1
-
|X d|
number of states
per process
8 (temperatures)
7 (light intensities)
-
pX
state change rate
0.01
2−9 to 2−2
Cerasure
data
data channel
erasure rate
0.01
0.00 to 0.99
we
energy weight
1 1
J
2−3 to 2−5
TABLE II: Simulation Parameters
ESP-IDF environment, combining high-level Arduino APIs
with low-level ESP32 functionality. The Arduino framework is
primarily used for serial communication and timing functions,
while the ESP-IDF headers provide access to BLE stack
configuration and control. The firmware is structured as a main
event loop with asynchronous callbacks for BLE and timer
events, executing on the FreeRTOS-based ESP-IDF runtime.
VII. EVALUATION
In this section, we present a numerical and experimental
evaluation of our PSBO approach against baseline strategies.
Sec. VII-A describes the simulation parameters, while Sec.
VII-B details the energy measurements used to set these
parameters based on real hardware. Sec. VII-C presents the
simulation results, and Sec. VII-D reports results of the
experimental analysis.
A. Simulation Setup
In a first step, we simulated the model described in Sec.
III numerically. Simulation parameters are listed in Table II.
The transition probabilities of the Markov chain C modeling
the channel erasure rates and delays are derived from data
obtained through the hardware setup described above in Sec.
VI. To this end, we logged a full day of delay values for every
time step and quantized the resulting delays into 20ms bins.
The occurrences of transitions between these bins then directly
translate to the transition probabilities in C.
We aim to cover a large span of possible parameters to an-
alyze the performance of PSBO under diverse circumstances.
To this end, we vary the data channel erasure rate Cerasure
data
from 0.00 to 0.99. In the latter case, only 1% of transmission
attempts succeed. The energy weight we takes values from
2−4, where data quality degradation is contributing a signif-
icantly larger share to the objective up to 24, where energy
consumption dominates the cost. The process state change rate
pX describes how fast the observed process changes its state.
We vary it from 2−9, which corresponds to processes that are
nearly static and change every 8.5 minutes on average to 2−2,
which corresponds to processes that are highly volatile and
change every 4 seconds on average. The varying parameters
are listed in gray rows in Table II, where the values indicated
correspond to the fixed values used when varying the other
parameters.
To quantify data quality degradation, we use the GoT metric.
To recall the meaning of data quality in this context, assume
the simulation setting wqual = 1, we = 1 1
J. Then, a data
quality cost value of 1 contributes one cost unit to the objective
function and is therefore treated as equivalent to an energy cost
of 1 J.
We use two variants of the GoT. The first considered GoT,
GoT A, partitions the process states into critical and non-
critical ones. If the observed process is in a critical state
Xt while the receiver indicates a non-critical state XRx
t
, the
resulting data quality is substantially lower than for any other
inconsistency, and the corresponding costs increase linearly
with AoII. All other state inconsistencies yield a data quality
that decreases linearly with AoII with a small scaling factor,
here set to 0.001. With GoT B, we illustrate that our approach
can accommodate arbitrary definitions of data quality and
is not dependent on patterns like in GoT A. To construct
GoT B, each state pair (Xt, XRx
t
) is assigned a random base
data quality drawn uniformly from [−0.5 −v, −0.5 + v],
which is then decreased linearly with increasing AoII. The
corresponding slope is scaled by an independent random factor
uniformly distributed in [0.5 −v, 0.5 + v], where v controls
the variability of assigned costs.
We evaluate PSBO against five baseline strategies. The
Random strategy always decides to enter deep-sleep mode
after the IoT device was awake for one time step. It chooses
a deep-sleep duration uniformly at random between 30 and
300 seconds, independent of the system’s state. The Al-
waysTransmit strategy remains continuously active, sensing
and transmitting at every time step. This guarantees the highest
data quality and results in the highest energy consumption.
Conversely, the NeverTransmit strategy stays in deep-sleep
mode for the entire time and completely omits communication.
This strategy minimizes energy consumption but suffers from
minimal data quality. The Threshold strategy transmits only
when a predefined AoI threshold θ is exceeded. We choose
θ such that this strategy optimizes the AoI-case as derived in
[2]:
θ =
$s
2we · (T s · P s + T w · P w + T Tx · P a)
wqual
%
+ 1 (15)
This strategy is optimal under traditional AoI-based metrics,
but it neglects application-specific data quality and makes no
use of context-specific information. The Q-Learning strategy
adaptively selects sleep durations using tabular reinforcement
learning. It learns to minimize a long-term cost function that
includes both energy and data quality. To make tabular learning
possible, we use discrete states for Q-Learning which consist


--- Page 11 ---
11
0.0
0.2
0.4
0.6
0.8
Data Channel Erasure Rate Cdata
erasure
10−2
10−1
100
101
102
Average Cost cπ
avg
AlwaysTransmit
NeverTransmit
PSBO
Q-Learning
Random
Threshold [2]
(a) Varying data channel erasure rates
2−3
2−2
2−1
20
21
22
23
24
25
Energy Weight we
10−2
10−1
100
101
102
Average Cost cπ
avg
AlwaysTransmit
NeverTransmit
PSBO
Q-Learning
Random
Threshold [2]
(b) Varying energy weights
Fig. 5: Average cost of PSBO and baselines for varying parameters (GoT A)
2−9
2−8
2−7
2−6
2−5
2−4
2−3
2−2
State Change Rate pX
10−2
10−1
100
101
102
Average Cost cπ
avg
AlwaysTransmit
NeverTransmit
PSBO
Q-Learning
Random
Threshold [2]
Fig. 6: Average cost of PSBO and baselines for varying state
change rates (GoT A)
of XTx, XRx, AoITx, AoIRx, and AoII.
B. Energy Measurements
For the simulations, we assume energy consumption values
corresponding to the real energy consumption used by a setup
described in Sec. VI. To obtain these values, the energy
consumption of the sensor node is measured using the Power
Profiler Kit 2 (PPK2) by Nordic Semiconductor [28]. The
device is tested during deep-sleep, and during the wake-up
phase, the sensing phase, the transmission/listening phase, and
the idle phase. In each case, we measure the average current
Iavg drawn from a 5 V supply. Iavg is then multiplied by the
supply voltage to obtain the mean power draw P in Watt.
Table III summarizes the measured current and correspond-
ing energy consumption for each mode. All measurements
were performed in a stable laboratory environment.
C. Simulation Results
To evaluate the performance of PSBO, we first conduct
a series of numerical experiments covering diverse system
configurations. The corresponding outcomes are presented in
Parameter
Iavg [mA]
P [W]
P d
0.97338
0.00487
P w
38.25
0.19125
P a
101.51
0.50755
P i
27.59
0.13795
P s
28.69
0.14345
TABLE III: Measured current and energy consumption for
each operation mode.
Fig. 5 to Fig. 7. Results for GoT A are reported in Fig. 5 and
Fig. 6. Here, we assume that the device measures temperatures.
We further assume a fixed state change rate of 0.01. Critical
states are temperatures below 0◦C. The results for GoT B are
presented in Fig. 7a to Fig. 7b. Here, we assume that the
device measures light intensities with variable state change
rates derived from our own measurements in the same fashion
as described above for the delays in C. In all figures, we
show a shadow behind the mean values including 50% of all
outcomes ranging from the 0.25-quantile to the 0.75-quantile
of all outcomes.
Fig. 5a illustrates the average cost cπ
avg for different data
channel erasure rates Cdata
erasure using GoT A. The erasure rate
is held constant during each evaluation. PSBO outperforms
all baselines across the entire range. At a low erasure rate
of 0.01, PSBO incurs a cost of 0.02. Q-Learning results in
a cost of 0.07, whereas AlwaysTransmit and Threshold result
in significantly higher costs of 0.23. Under more challenging
conditions such as Cdata
erasure = 0.75, the performance gap
widens, with PSBO maintaining a cost of 0.06 in contrast
to 0.55 for Q-Learning and 2.38 for Threshold.
Fig. 5b presents the average cost as a function of the
energy weight we, which is varied from 2−3 to 25. Across all
regimes, PSBO achieves the most favorable tradeoff between
data quality degradation and energy consumption. At low
energy emphasis (we = 2−3), PSBO incurs a cost of 0.006,
whereas AlwaysTransmit and Threshold yield 0.03 and 0.04,
respectively. At we = 20, PSBO remains low at 0.02, in
contrast to Threshold’s 0.23. For the extreme case we = 25,
PSBO incurs a cost of 0.33, remaining significantly below

Fig. 7: Average cost of PSBO and baselines for a varying parameters (GoT B)

Average Data Quality Cost cπ
util
(c) Comparison of average data quality costs
Fig. 8: Experimental Results for our PSBO approach and baseline strategies
Q-Learning (0.43) and Threshold (4.00). This demonstrates
effective adaptation under increasing energy penalties.
Fig. 6 shows the impact of varying state change rates pX
on the average cost. Here, we assume that the probability
of the process to stay in the current state is 1 −pX and
that the probability to change to an adjacent state is given
by pX divided by the number of adjacent states. As pX
increases from 2−9 to 2−2, PSBO retains the lowest cost
across all settings. At the lowest transition rate, PSBO yields
a cost of 0.01, compared to 0.02 for Q-Learning and 0.05 for
Threshold. As state change rates increase, energy consumption
rises for adaptive schemes. At pX = 2−2, PSBO results in
a cost of 0.12, while the costs incurred by Q-Learning and
Threshold increase to 0.60 and 1.47, respectively. In contrast,
the outcome of AlwaysTransmit remains unaffected, while
NeverTransmit and Random occasionally benefit from high
volatility due to the process returning to its initial state, which
results in lower costs from data quality degradation.
Fig. 7a provides the results for N = 100 runs, where in each
run, we randomly choose one of three randomly generated
GoTs using the scheme GoT B with v = 0.1, 0.2, 0.3 under
increasing Cdata
erasure. The overall trends remain consistent with
those observed for GoT A, with PSBO yielding the lowest
average costs throughout. At Cdata
erasure = 0.01, the cost is
0.07, while Q-Learning and Threshold yield 0.31 and 0.69,
respectively. At 0.75, PSBO remains at 0.28, outperforming
Q-Learning (1.03) and Threshold (1.96).
Fig. 7b reports results for varying we using GoT B. The
behavior for randomly generated GoTs mirrors that seen for
GoT A. At we = 2−3, PSBO yields a low cost of 0.02,
compared to AlwaysTransmit (0.03) and Threshold (0.28). For
we = 20, PSBO continues to perform well at 0.07, while
baseline costs grow steeply. At we = 25, the cost incurred by
PSBO increases to 0.67, still undercutting Q-Learning (0.80)
and Threshold (2.94).
D. Experimental Results
In Fig. 8, we present the average cost obtained by the eval-
uated strategies for experiments of 24 hours for each strategy
and three fixed instances of GoT B. Experiments for the Nev-
erTransmit strategy effectively correspond to only measuring
the deep-sleep energy of the device, which is why we exclude
them here. The results show that PSBO achieves the lowest
total cost among all compared approaches at 0.39 compared
to 0.88, 0.96, 3.54, and 5.26 for AlwaysTransmit, Threshold,
Q-learning, and Random, respectively. These results differ
from those in the simulated environment for several reasons.
First, the number of runs per strategy is lower due to the
significantly higher runtime needed per experiment compared
to simulations. With fewer runs, the estimated performance
becomes noisier because there is less averaging, so results can
shift more due to chance and outliers. Second, the difference
is also due to higher and more volatile channel erasure rates,
which are assumed to be fixed in the simulations. This results
in a more challenging environment for PSBO and Q-learning.
Periods of outages lead to higher standard deviations, which
becomes apparent, for example, when comparing the data
quality degradation cost of AlwaysTransmit, which is even
higher than that of PSBO, as continuous synchronization
is prevented by outages. AlwaysTransmit still exhibits low


--- Page 13 ---
13
data quality degradation cost (0.68) compared to reference
strategies. This behavior is accompanied by the highest energy
cost (0.20) among the evaluated approaches, which results in
a higher total cost than our PSBO approach. The energy cost
for AlwaysTransmit are more than four times higher than those
for PSBO. Random and Threshold achieve lower energy costs
of approx. 0.01. However, this leads to higher data quality
degradation costs (5.25 and 0.95) due to the increased AoII
at the receiver, which dominates their total cost. Q-learning
converges too slowly to yield competitive performance within
the considered time horizon, even after optimizing the state
space to accelerate its learning. While it reduces data quality
degradation costs compared to Random, it is not able to reduce
the cost as much as PSBO, AlwaysTransmit or Threshold.
PSBO finds a better tradeoff between energy consumption and
data quality degradation cost than all the baseline schemes.
VIII. CONCLUSIONS
In this work, we introduced PSBO, a novel algorithm for
deep-sleep scheduling in S-IoT, which optimizes the tradeoff
between energy consumption at the IoT device and data
quality degradation at the receiver. By using the GoT met-
ric, PSBO provides application-specific scheduling decisions
that are aware of the usefulness of transmitted information.
We formulated a corresponding MDP and designed PSBO
to predict future costs under uncertain channel and process
dynamics. Extensive numerical simulations demonstrated that
PSBO consistently outperforms baseline methods, including a
threshold-based agent and Q-learning, across a wide range of
channel conditions, energy weightings, and process dynamics.
We additionally verified the applicability of our approach and
its superior performance under realistic conditions using an S-
IoT testbed. Our results show the potential of the GoT metric
in the IoT, especially when energy consumption is a critical
concern. Future work may for example explore the integration
of multi-device coordination.
REFERENCES
[1] X. Wang, X. Li, M. Zhu, Q. Gao, Y. Huo, and T. Jing, “On pursuit
of sleep-scheduling for sensor network in IIoT: A non-linear AoI
optimization perspective,” in Proc. of 16th International Conference on
Communication Software and Networks, 2024.
[2] X. Cao, J. Wang, Y. Cheng, and J. Jin, “Optimal sleep scheduling for
energy-efficient aoi optimization in industrial internet of things,” IEEE
Internet of Things Journal, 2023.
[3] J. Gong, J. Zhu, X. Chen, X. Ma, and C. M. C., “Sleep, sense or transmit:
Energy-age tradeoff for status update with two-threshold optimal policy,”
IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications, 2022.
[4] L. Badia and A. Munari, “Satellite intermittent connectivity and its
impact on age of information for finite horizon scheduling,” in Proc. of
12th Advanced Satellite Multimedia Systems Conference and the 18th
Signal Processing for Space Communications Workshop, 2025.
[5] S. Dongare, A. Jovovic, W. de Sombre, A. Ortiz, and A. Klein,
“Minimizing the age of incorrect information for status update systems
with energy harvesting,” in Proc. of IEEE International Conference on
Communications, 2024.
[6] Y. Chen and A. Ephremides, “Minimizing age of incorrect information
for unreliable channel with power constraint,” in Proc. of IEEE Global
Communications Conference, 2021.
[7] ——, “Minimizing age of incorrect information over a channel with
random delay,” IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking, 2024.
[8] C. Kam, S. Kompella, and A. Ephremides, “Age of incorrect information
for remote estimation of a binary markov source,” in Proc. of IEEE
Conference on Computer Communications Workshops, 2020.
[9] W. de Sombre, S. Dongare, A. Klein, and A. Ortiz, “Minimizing the
age of incorrect information with continual belief learning,” in Proc. of
IEEE International Conference on Communications, 2025.
[10] S. Kriouile and M. Assaad, “Minimizing the age of incorrect
information for unknown markovian source,” 2022. [Online]. Available:
https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.09681
[11] A. Maatouk, M. Assaad, and A. Ephremides, “Semantics-empowered
communications through the age of incorrect information,” in Proc. of
IEEE International Conference on Communications, 2022.
[12] W. de Sombre, F. Marques, F. Pyttel, A. Ortiz, and A. Klein, “A unified
approach to learn transmission strategies using age-based metrics in
point-to-point wireless communication,” Proc. of IEEE Global Com-
munications Conference, 2023.

of incorrect information minimization for semantic-empowered NOMA
system in S-IoT,” IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications,
2024.

of incorrect information minimization for timely status updates in
satellite-based IoT,” in Proc. of IEEE/CIC International Conference on
Communications in China, 2024.
[15] O. Guersoy and N. Akar, “Energy-age of incorrect information trade-off
with timer-based sleep-wake scheduling,” 2024. [Online]. Available:
http://dx.doi.org/10.36227/techrxiv.172503953.39448282/v1

design for freshness-critical satellite-IoT systems,” IEEE Internet of
Things Journal, 2020.

and satellite-based m2m/iot services in support of smart grids,” Mobile
Networks and Applications, 2018.

murthy, “IoT enabled power quality monitoring in substations through
LoRa LEO satellite communication,” in Proc. of 12th Electrical Power,
Electronics, Communications, Controls and Informatics Seminar, 2024.

one update?” in Proc. of IEEE Int. Conf. Computer Commun., 2012.
[20] I. Kadota, A. Sinha, E. Uysal-Biyikoglu, R. Singh, and E. Modiano,
“Scheduling policies for minimizing age of information in broadcast
wireless networks,” IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking, 2018.

Incorrect Information: A New Performance Metric for Status Updates,”
IEEE/ACM Transactions on Networking, 2020.

oriented semantic communications: New metrics, framework, and open
challenges,” IEEE Wireless Communications, 2024.
[23] S. Heine, C. Hofmann, and A. Knopp, “End-to-end latency evaluation of
leo satellite iot systems,” in Proc. of 40th International Communications
Satellite Systems Conference, 2023.
[24] E. Kim, I. P. Roberts, and J. G. Andrews, “Downlink analysis and
evaluation of multi-beam LEO satellite communication in shadowed
rician channels,” IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology, 2024.

mization: a review of algorithms and applications,” Annals of Operations
Research, 2016.

model for radio communication channels,” IEEE Transactions on Ve-
hicular Technology, 1995.
